Node.js Basics, Part 18: Template Engines — EJS vs. Pug
A new chapter begins. Next up is templates — a really common technique in development — so let me introduce it here.

In the past, frontend development meant static HTML pages. But if you had 100 pages and one day needed to tweak the header, you'd suddenly have 100 pages to edit. That's super inefficient, which is why templates came along. So what are the common templates out there in the industry? Here are the two main ones.

If you're just starting out, I'd recommend EJS, since it's not that different from writing HTML — so the learning curve is lower. Being a template language, it lets you write variables, loops, or set up layouts. For example, to pass in a variable or write code, you wrap it in
That represents a block of code.
Each tag form is slightly different — you can check the

If we compare EJS and Pug, it's a bit like SCSS vs. SASS — everyone knows SASS has a steeper learning curve than SCSS, and Pug is the same. Pug's syntax is a little different and very terse; code blocks have no special markers, and variables use the equals sign (=):

ul
  each val in [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]
    li= val

Both templates are worth getting familiar with — each has its pros and cons.
